author: @andymiddleton6431
text:
Hey Robert, love all the stuff you investigate and share, makes one think a lot!!
Seeing you stick weld the chain, I wonder if you’ve done TIG welding, i can imagine the huge clean up that you had to do stick welding all that chain, IMHO TIG would have been probably faster and definitely a lot cleaner... i put off learning it for years and regret not doing it earlier! 😜

author: @totherarf
text:
You may have wear problems with that setup Rob. Welding the chain will screw with the individual rollers in each link and stop the sprocket "rolling" round each one. It will still work though!

It seems a bit sad to have a hubleess wheel with an alternative "hub" all but in the same position. How about having a set of rollers to go round the outside of the wheel to prevent it pushing away from the sprocket? The rollers against the side of the wheel would need to be fairly close tolerance but the tyre would allow a looser setup on that set of rollers! ... Just a thought ;o)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: all good thoughts mate - I liked this as an alternative but let's face it there is never an ideal solution lol

author: @kenrowe167
text: Hi Rob. This is certainly more robust than the toothed belt idea. But the toothed belt idea is great for making templates for cutting metal gears (without needing a lathe etc.). I think you would only need an angle grinder and a few files. This is great, because I now (at last) have a method for cutting elliptical gears, which I need for a project I'm working on - thanks to you!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: awesome mate - I agree the belt idea works on lots of levels and this is one stop answer - but I did like it which is why I did it - I thought it needed a bit more exposure than a suggestion in the comments

You'd need to use thin rods at a low setting, but I don't see why it couldn't work.

author: @alaingrignon
text: @Rob you mentioned using this for a wind turbine along with the planetary gears. I've been thinking alot about the washing machine vertical turbine you built with all the microwave bits and magnets along the rim. When you showed the planetary gear system I thought it would be really interesting if you attached it to that VWT then place a larger universal motor to the central cog. That way it would generate from all the magnets along the edge, and when it really blows, the universal motor in the center would also pick-up extra watts and capture more of the wind energy at minimal added cost. Would love to see the results of that!
